question: [File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits] Can an unprivileged attacker who intercepts a previously broadcast but unsubmitted EcrecoverAuthorizer authorization signature replay it after the authorizer has already incremented their nonce via a direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized call, causing nonce[authorizer] to be ahead of the replayed authorization.nonce and permanently blocking the pending signature from being submitted, freezing the delegated authorization flow that MidnightBundles relies on to call consumableUnits on behalf of the taker? Preconditions: authorizer has submitted one direct setIsAuthorized on Midnight, nonce[authorizer] == 1, attacker holds a signed Authorization with nonce==0. Call sequence: (1) authorizer calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized directly, (2) attacker attempts EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with nonce==0 Authorization, (3) call reverts with InvalidNonce, (4) call consumableUnits for an offer whose maker == authorizer. Invariant tested: nonce monotonicity must not allow a valid pending signature to be permanently invalidated by an out-of-band state change. Scoped impact: delegated authorization freeze; MidnightBundles cannot compute consumable units for affected maker offers. Proof idea: unit test asserting that a signed Authorization with nonce==0 reverts after a direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized call increments the effective nonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit a valid EcrecoverAuthorizer authorization signature at exactly block.timestamp == authorization.deadline (the boundary condition in `require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline)`) to grant or revoke authorization one block later than the maker intended, allowing the attacker to time the submission to coincide with a favorable market state and then immediately call setConsumed via the newly authorized address to manipulate consumed[maker][group], causing consumableUnits to return an inflated remaining capacity? Preconditions: attacker holds a signed Authorization with deadline D, block.timestamp == D. Call sequence: (1) warp to block.timestamp == D, (2) submit EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, (3) authorized address calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, 0, maker) — reverts because 0 < consumed, (4) authorized address calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker), (5) consumableUnits returns maxUnits or maxAssets-based value as if offer is fresh. Invariant tested: deadline must be exclusive or the inclusive boundary must not be exploitable for timing attacks. Scoped impact: offer overfill via artificially reset consumed state. Proof idea: fuzz deadline boundary ±1 block and assert consumed state cannot be reset by a freshly authorized address.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setConsumed] Can an authorized operator who was granted authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel all offers in a group, causing ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits to return 0 for every offer in that group, effectively DoS-ing MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral for any taker relying on those offers? Preconditions: attacker is authorized for maker via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, maker has active offers with shared group. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker), (2) consumableUnits reads consumed[maker][group] == type(uint256).max, (3) zeroFloorSub(maxUnits, type(uint256).max) == 0, (4) MidnightBundles loop skips all offers, (5) require(filledUnits == targetUnits) reverts with OutOfOffers. Invariant tested: authorized operators must not be able to unilaterally cancel maker offers without maker consent. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS of taker flows via consumed state manipulation. Proof idea: unit test asserting that setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max) by authorized address causes consumableUnits to return 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer's domain separator uses block.chainid (computed at call time) while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID (captured at construction) to replay an authorization signature from the pre-fork chain on the post-fork chain, granting themselves authorization over a maker's Midnight position on the forked chain, then calling setConsumed to manipulate consumed state read by consumableUnits? Preconditions: hard fork changes block.chainid, attacker holds a pre-fork signed Authorization, post-fork EcrecoverAuthorizer uses new chainid so domain separator differs — but wait, if chainid changes, the domain separator changes too, so replay is blocked. However: if the fork does NOT change chainid (e.g., a chain split where both chains keep the same chainid), the domain separator is identical on both chains. Call sequence: (1) maker signs Authorization on chain A with chainid=1, (2) chain splits, both keep chainid=1, (3) attacker submits Authorization on chain B, (4) nonce[authorizer] on chain B is still 0 (no prior submissions), (5) authorization succeeds, (6) attacker calls setConsumed. Invariant tested: EIP-712 domain must uniquely bind signatures to a single chain deployment. Scoped impact: cross-chain authorization replay enabling consumed state manipulation. Proof idea: differential test simulating two chains with same chainid and asserting authorization cannot be replayed.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an authorized operator (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, maker) to re-authorize the EcrecoverAuthorizer contract on behalf of the maker, then use a previously signed but nonce-stale Authorization to re-grant authorization to themselves after the maker has revoked it, because the nonce in EcrecoverAuthorizer is per-authorizer and the operator can call setIsAuthorized on Midnight directly to reset the authorization chain? Preconditions: maker authorized operator via EcrecoverAuthorizer (nonce==0→1), maker revoked operator via Midnight.setIsAuthorized, operator holds a signed Authorization with nonce==1. Call sequence: (1) operator calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, maker) — succeeds because operator is still authorized on Midnight, (2) operator submits EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with nonce==1 Authorization granting themselves authorization again, (3) operator now authorized again. Invariant tested: authorization revocation must be permanent unless maker explicitly re-grants. Scoped impact: unauthorized re-authorization enabling consumed state manipulation via setConsumed. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting that revoked operator cannot re-authorize themselves.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a maker's EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized submission by observing the pending transaction in the mempool, extracting the Authorization struct and Signature, and submitting it first (since the function is permissionless — no msg.sender check), causing the nonce to increment before the maker's intended submission, making the maker's transaction revert with InvalidNonce and leaving the authorization in an unintended state? Preconditions: maker broadcasts EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized transaction, attacker observes mempool. Call sequence: (1) attacker front-runs with identical Authorization and Signature, (2) nonce[authorizer] increments to 1, (3) maker's transaction reverts with InvalidNonce, (4) if the authorization was a revocation, the target remains authorized. Invariant tested: permissionless submission must not allow front-running to block or delay authorization changes. Scoped impact: authorization freeze or delayed revocation enabling continued consumed state manipulation. Proof idea: unit test simulating front-run by submitting authorization from a different address before the maker.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits] Can an attacker who is authorized for a maker call EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel a valid offer root, causing all offers in that Merkle tree to fail ratification, while consumableUnits still reads consumed[maker][group] as non-zero (from prior fills), making the remaining capacity appear non-zero but take() will always revert with RatifierFail, creating a state where consumableUnits returns positive values that are permanently unreachable? Preconditions: maker has active offers under a Merkle root, attacker is authorized for maker on Midnight. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls EcrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root), (2) consumableUnits(midnight, id, offer) returns positive value, (3) MidnightBundles calls take() with that value, (4) take() calls isRatified which reverts with RootCanceled, (5) catch{} swallows revert, (6) filledUnits never reaches targetUnits, (7) OutOfOffers revert. Invariant tested: consumableUnits must accurately reflect whether an offer can actually be filled. Scoped impact: DoS of MidnightBundles flows; consumableUnits returns misleading positive capacity. Proof idea: integration test asserting that cancelRoot by authorized address causes MidnightBundles to revert with OutOfOffers.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer's AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH encodes `Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)` but does NOT include a `bytes32 salt` or market-scoped field, allowing a single signed Authorization to be submitted multiple times across different EcrecoverAuthorizer deployments (if multiple instances exist) to grant authorization on each, since the domain separator only binds to `chainId` and `verifyingContract` but a maker might sign for one instance and an attacker submits to another? Preconditions: multiple EcrecoverAuthorizer instances deployed on same chain, maker signs for instance A, attacker submits to instance B. Call sequence: (1) maker signs Authorization for EcrecoverAuthorizer_A, (2) attacker submits to EcrecoverAuthorizer_B — domain separator differs (different verifyingContract), so this fails. BUT: if maker signs with wrong verifyingContract address (e.g., off-by-one deployment), attacker can submit to the correct instance. Invariant tested: EIP-712 domain must uniquely bind to the intended verifyingContract. Scoped impact: cross-instance authorization replay. Proof idea: differential test with two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances asserting signatures are not cross-replayable.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer checks `signer == authorization.authorizer || IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer)` to submit an Authorization signed by an authorized operator (not the authorizer themselves), where the Authorization grants the attacker authorization, and then the authorizer revokes the operator — but the submitted Authorization already incremented the nonce, so the authorizer cannot use the same nonce to revoke the attacker's authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized operator, operator signs Authorization granting attacker authorization, attacker submits it. Call sequence: (1) operator signs Authorization{authorizer=maker, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}, (2) attacker submits EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, (3) nonce[maker] becomes 1, (4) maker tries to revoke via EcrecoverAuthorizer with nonce=1 but operator is already revoked on Midnight, (5) maker must use nonce=1 to revoke attacker. Invariant tested: operator-signed authorizations must not create irrevocable authorization chains. Scoped impact: unauthorized persistent authorization enabling consumed state manipulation. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting that operator-signed authorization can be revoked by maker.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits / Midnight.consumed] Can an attacker who gains authorization via a replayed or front-run EcrecoverAuthorizer signature call Midnight.setConsumed(group, currentConsumed - 1, maker) — which reverts because amount < consumed — and then call setConsumed(group, currentConsumed, maker) as a no-op, confirming that consumed state cannot be decreased, but then exploit the fact that setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) sets consumed to max, causing consumableUnits to return 0 for all offers in the group, while the attacker's own offers in the same group are unaffected (different maker address)? Preconditions: attacker authorized for maker, maker has offers with shared group, attacker has separate offers in same group. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker), (2) consumableUnits for maker's offers returns 0, (3) consumableUnits for attacker's offers returns full capacity. Invariant tested: setConsumed must not allow authorized operators to selectively cancel competitor offers. Scoped impact: targeted DoS of specific maker's offers while preserving attacker's own capacity. Proof idea: unit test asserting that setConsumed(max) by authorized address zeroes consumableUnits for victim maker.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash] Can an attacker craft a ratifierData where the proof array has a different length than expected, causing HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length) to return a different typehash, and the EIP-712 digest to be computed over a structHash that does not match what the maker signed, allowing the attacker to forge a valid-looking ratification for an offer the maker never intended to authorize, bypassing the signature check? Preconditions: attacker controls ratifierData encoding, maker signed a root for a tree of height H. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits ratifierData with proof of length H+1 (padding with zero sibling), (2) offerTreeTypeHash(H+1) returns different hash, (3) digest differs from what maker signed, (4) ecrecover returns wrong address or address(0), (5) require(_signer != address(0)) reverts. Invariant tested: proof length must be bound to the signed typehash to prevent typehash confusion. Scoped impact: ratifier bypass attempt; if ecrecover returns a valid authorized address by coincidence, offer is filled without maker consent. Proof idea: fuzz proof.length and assert that only the correct height produces a valid signature.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the absence of a `name` and `version` field in EcrecoverAuthorizer's EIP-712 domain separator (`EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)`) to create a collision with another contract on the same chain that uses the same minimal domain typehash and address, allowing a signature intended for that other contract to be replayed against EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant unauthorized authorization? Preconditions: another contract on same chain uses identical EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH and happens to be deployed at the same address (counterfactual), or a future upgrade deploys EcrecoverAuthorizer at a previously used address. Call sequence: (1) maker signs a message for contract X at address A, (2) EcrecoverAuthorizer is deployed at address A (e.g., via CREATE2 after selfdestruct), (3) attacker replays signature against EcrecoverAuthorizer. Invariant tested: domain separator must uniquely identify the contract and its purpose. Scoped impact: cross-contract signature replay enabling unauthorized authorization. Proof idea: unit test asserting that domain separator is unique per deployment address.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits] Can an attacker manipulate the consumed[maker][group] state by gaining authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer and calling setConsumed to set consumed to exactly maxAssets - 1 (for an assets-based offer), causing consumableUnits to call buyerAssetsToUnits or sellerAssetsToUnits with targetAssets == 1, which due to mulDivUp rounding returns 1 unit, allowing MidnightBundles to attempt a 1-unit take that may succeed or fail depending on tick/price, creating a dust-level fill that wastes gas and corrupts the expected fill accounting? Preconditions: attacker authorized for maker, offer has maxAssets > 0, consumed[maker][group] == maxAssets - 1. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls setConsumed(group, maxAssets - 1, maker), (2) consumableUnits calls buyerAssetsToUnits(midnight, id, offer, 1), (3) mulDivUp(1, WAD, buyerPrice) returns 1 unit, (4) MidnightBundles calls take with 1 unit, (5) take succeeds, consumed becomes maxAssets. Invariant tested: consumed state manipulation must not allow dust-level fills that corrupt accounting. Scoped impact: accounting corruption via dust fill; consumed state desync from maker intent. Proof idea: fuzz consumed values near maxAssets and assert consumableUnits returns expected remaining capacity.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is authorized for a maker (via EcrecoverAuthorizer) call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker) to grant themselves direct authorization on Midnight (bypassing EcrecoverAuthorizer's nonce mechanism), and then use this direct authorization to call setConsumed or other state-changing functions without any nonce or signature check, making the EcrecoverAuthorizer's nonce-based revocation mechanism ineffective? Call sequence: (1) attacker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker) — succeeds because attacker is authorized, (2) maker revokes attacker via EcrecoverAuthorizer (nonce increments), (3) attacker is still directly authorized on Midnight, (4) attacker calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker). Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce-based revocation must be sufficient to fully revoke all derived authorizations. Scoped impact: persistent unauthorized authorization after EcrecoverAuthorizer revocation. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting that EcrecoverAuthorizer revocation does not remove direct Midnight authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits] Can an attacker observe that consumableUnits reads consumed[offer.maker][offer.group] at call time (view function, no state change), and exploit the fact that between the consumableUnits call in MidnightBundles and the subsequent take() call, another transaction can increment consumed[maker][group] (via a competing take or setConsumed), causing the actual take to revert with ConsumedUnits or ConsumedAssets, while MidnightBundles silently catches the revert and continues, potentially leaving the taker with fewer filled units than expected? Preconditions: maker has an offer with remaining capacity, attacker can front-run MidnightBundles transaction. Call sequence: (1) MidnightBundles computes unitsToTake via consumableUnits, (2) attacker front-runs with a take that fills the remaining capacity, (3) MidnightBundles calls take with stale unitsToTake, (4) take reverts with ConsumedUnits, (5) catch{} swallows revert, (6) filledUnits < targetUnits, (7) OutOfOffers revert. Invariant tested: consumableUnits staleness must not cause silent accounting errors in MidnightBundles. Scoped impact: taker receives fewer units than expected; potential fund loss if maxBuyerAssets was already pulled. Proof idea: integration test simulating front-run between consumableUnits read and take execution.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified checks `!isRootCanceled[offer.maker][root]` but does NOT check if the maker's authorization of the EcrecoverRatifier contract on Midnight has been revoked, allowing a maker who has revoked EcrecoverRatifier's authorization on Midnight to still have their offers filled (since isRatified is called by Midnight which checks `isAuthorized[offer.maker][offer.ratifier]` before calling isRatified), but the reverse: a maker who never authorized EcrecoverRatifier cannot have their offers filled even if a valid signature exists? Preconditions: maker has valid signed root, maker has NOT called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverRatifier, true, maker). Call sequence: (1) take() is called with offer.ratifier == ecrecoverRatifier, (2) Midnight checks isAuthorized[maker][ecrecoverRatifier] — false, (3) reverts with RatifierUnauthorized before isRatified is called, (4) consumableUnits returns positive value but take always reverts. Invariant tested: consumableUnits must account for ratifier authorization status. Scoped impact: consumableUnits returns misleading positive capacity for offers with unauthorized ratifier. Proof idea: unit test asserting that consumableUnits returns positive but take reverts when ratifier is not authorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the nonce increment pattern `authorization.nonce == nonce[authorization.authorizer]++` where the nonce is incremented AFTER the equality check, to submit two transactions with the same nonce in the same block (if the first one is included first), causing the second to revert, but if the attacker can reorder transactions (e.g., via MEV), submit a revocation with nonce N and a grant with nonce N+1 in reverse order, causing the grant to revert and the revocation to succeed, or vice versa? Preconditions: attacker controls transaction ordering (MEV), maker has signed both a grant (nonce=N) and a revocation (nonce=N+1). Call sequence: (1) attacker reorders: submit revocation (nonce=N+1) first — reverts with InvalidNonce, (2) submit grant (nonce=N) — succeeds, (3) nonce becomes N+1, (4) revocation with nonce=N+1 can now be submitted. Invariant tested: nonce ordering must prevent MEV-based reordering of authorization changes. Scoped impact: authorization state manipulation via transaction reordering. Proof idea: state-transition test simulating out-of-order submission of grant and revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits / TakeAmountsLib.buyerAssetsToUnits] Can an attacker who manipulates consumed[maker][group] via authorized setConsumed to be exactly maxAssets cause consumableUnits to call buyerAssetsToUnits(midnight, id, offer, 0) (since zeroFloorSub(maxAssets, maxAssets) == 0), which returns mulDivUp(0, WAD, buyerPrice) == 0, causing MidnightBundles to call take with 0 units, which is a no-op take that still calls the maker's callback (per Midnight's comment 'Midnight can call the callback of offers through a no-op take, even if those offers have consumed==max'), potentially triggering unintended callback execution? Preconditions: attacker authorized for maker, offer has maxAssets > 0 and callback != address(0), consumed[maker][group] == maxAssets. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls setConsumed(group, maxAssets, maker), (2) consumableUnits returns 0, (3) MidnightBundles calls min(targetUnits - filledUnits, takes[i].units, 0) == 0, (4) take(offer, ratifierData, 0, taker, ...) is called, (5) maker callback is triggered with 0 units. Invariant tested: 0-unit takes must not trigger unintended callbacks or state changes. Scoped impact: unintended callback execution via consumed state manipulation. Proof idea: unit test asserting that consumableUnits == 0 prevents MidnightBundles from calling take.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer calls `IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized(authorization.authorized, authorization.isAuthorized, authorization.authorizer)` where the `onBehalf` parameter is `authorization.authorizer`, and Midnight checks `onBehalf == msg.sender || isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender]`, meaning EcrecoverAuthorizer must be authorized by the authorizer on Midnight — but if EcrecoverAuthorizer is NOT authorized, the call reverts, yet the nonce has already been incremented (nonce[authorizer]++ happens before the Midnight call), causing a permanent nonce desync where the authorizer's nonce is incremented but the authorization was never applied? Preconditions: authorizer has NOT called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer), attacker submits a valid Authorization. Call sequence: (1) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized is called with valid sig and nonce==0, (2) deadline check passes, (3) nonce[authorizer]++ increments to 1, (4) Midnight.setIsAuthorized reverts with Unauthorized, (5) entire transaction reverts, (6) nonce is NOT incremented (revert undoes state). Invariant tested: nonce increment must be atomic with the Midnight call — verify that revert undoes nonce increment. Scoped impact: if nonce IS incremented before revert (it is not due to EVM revert semantics), permanent nonce desync. Proof idea: unit test asserting that a failed EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized does not increment nonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker craft a Merkle proof where leafIndex has bits set beyond proof.length (e.g., leafIndex = 2^proof.length + k), causing the `require(leafIndex >> proof.length == 0, LeafIndexOutOfRange())` check to revert, but if proof.length == 0 (single-offer tree), leafIndex must be 0, and an attacker submitting leafIndex == 1 with empty proof causes revert — confirming the check works — but what if proof.length == 256 and leafIndex is any uint256 value, causing `leafIndex >> 256` to be 0 in Solidity (shift by >= 256 is 0), allowing any leafIndex to pass the bounds check for a 256-height tree? Preconditions: attacker controls ratifierData, proof.length == 256 (but offerTreeTypeHash reverts for height > 20, so this is blocked). Verify: offerTreeTypeHash(256) reverts with TreeTooHigh, so proof.length > 20 is blocked. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits proof with length 21, (2) offerTreeTypeHash(21) reverts with TreeTooHigh, (3) isRatified reverts. Invariant tested: tree height cap must prevent leafIndex overflow in isLeaf. Scoped impact: ratifier bypass attempt via oversized proof. Proof idea: fuzz proof.length from 0 to 25 and assert TreeTooHigh for length > 20.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: ConsumableUnitsLib.consumableUnits] Can an attacker exploit the branch selection logic in consumableUnits — `if (offer.maxUnits > 0)` vs `else if (offer.buy)` vs `else` — by crafting an offer where both maxUnits == 0 and maxAssets == 0 (which Midnight allows as a no-op offer per 'No-ops are allowed'), causing consumableUnits to fall into the `else if (offer.buy)` branch and call buyerAssetsToUnits(midnight, id, offer, zeroFloorSub(0, consumed)) == buyerAssetsToUnits(midnight, id, offer, 0) == 0, returning 0 units, while the actual take with 0 units is a valid no-op that still triggers callbacks? Preconditions: offer has maxUnits == 0 and maxAssets == 0, offer.buy == true. Call sequence: (1) consumableUnits called with offer where maxUnits==0, maxAssets==0, buy==true, (2) falls into else-if branch, (3) buyerAssetsToUnits(midnight, id, offer, 0) returns 0, (4) MidnightBundles skips offer (unitsToTake == 0). Invariant tested: consumableUnits must correctly handle the zero-zero offer case without misleading callers. Scoped impact: silent skip of valid no-op offers in MidnightBundles. Proof idea: unit test with maxUnits==0, maxAssets==0 offer asserting consumableUnits returns 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/ConsumableUnitsLib.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who holds a signed Authorization with isAuthorized==false (a revocation) submit it after the authorizer has already revoked the target via a direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized call, causing the nonce to increment unnecessarily and consuming the authorizer's next valid nonce slot, potentially blocking a subsequent grant that the authorizer intended to submit with that nonce? Preconditions: authorizer has signed Authorization{isAuthorized=false, nonce=N}, authorizer has already called Midnight.setIsAuthorized(authorized, false, authorizer) directly, nonce[authorizer] == N. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits the revocation Authorization, (2) nonce[authorizer] becomes N+1, (3) Midnight.setIsAuthorized is called again (redundant), (4) authorizer's next intended Authorization with nonce=N+1 is now the correct nonce — no harm done.
